title: Control Flow: Teaching Code to Decide
learningObjectives: Write if/elif/else branches that choose behavior based on a stored value
Use comparison and boolean operators to express real thresholds, e.g. 'is demand above a safe limit'
Avoid the classic boundary mistake of using > when a limit should be inclusive (>=)
Combine multiple conditions with and/or without producing an unreadable one-liner
whyItMatters: The AI Energy OS constantly has to decide things: is this demand reading above a safe threshold, is the battery too low to discharge, is a forecast within a range the model trusts. Control flow is the mechanism for every one of those decisions, long before any ML model exists to make them for you.
capstoneContribution: Digital Twin threshold logic — the first branch of the overload/safety checks the Grid Copilot dashboard will later rely on.

problem.summary: A stored demand reading needs to trigger different behavior depending on its value.
problem.content: Your `demand_kw` variable (from the Variables lesson) holds `4821`. The line is only rated to `5000` kW. Right now your code has no way to notice it's getting close, let alone react differently depending on how close.

motivation.summary: Every 'smart' decision the platform ever makes starts as a rule you write by hand first.
motivation.content: Long before any model predicts anything, the system needs simple rules: is this value safe, high, or low. Control flow is how you express those rules today, and it's also the scaffolding every later mission's ML/RL decision-making eventually replaces or augments — you can't skip straight to 'smart' without first being able to write 'correct.'

theory.summary: if/elif/else runs exactly one branch, chosen by the first true condition.
theory.content: `if condition: ... elif condition: ... else: ...` executes exactly one branch — the first one whose condition is true, top to bottom — and skips the rest even if a later condition would also be true. Conditions are boolean expressions built from comparisons (`<`, `>`, `==`, `!=`, `<=`, `>=`) and boolean operators (`and`, `or`, `not`).

visualExplanation.summary: A decision diamond routes the demand value to exactly one of three labeled paths.
visualExplanation.content: The diagram shows the demand value flowing into a decision diamond ('> 4500?'), branching to a WARNING path or a NORMAL path, then merging back into the rest of the program — reinforcing visually that only one path is ever taken per run, never both.

interactiveDemo.summary: Drag demand_kw along a slider and watch the classification label update live.
interactiveDemo.content: A slider lets you drag `demand_kw` between 0 and 6000 and watch a NORMAL / WARNING / OVERLOAD label update instantly as your threshold code runs against it, including right at the boundary values.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: classify a demand reading against two thresholds.
guidedCoding.content:
```python
demand_kw = 4821
safe_limit_kw = 5000
warning_threshold_kw = 4500

if demand_kw >= safe_limit_kw:
    print("OVERLOAD")
elif demand_kw > warning_threshold_kw:
    print("WARNING")
else:
    print("NORMAL")
```
Run this, then try `demand_kw = 5000` exactly and confirm it prints `OVERLOAD` — notice the first condition uses `>=`, not `>`.

independentCoding.summary: Classify a battery charge level into three bands without a function yet.
independentCoding.content: Write a script that stores `battery_soc_percent` (0-100) and prints `CRITICAL` if below 10, `LOW` if below 25, else `OK`. Don't use a function — that's next lesson.

debugging.summary: Fix a boundary bug where a reading exactly at the limit slips through as safe.
debugging.content:
```python
if demand_kw > safe_limit_kw:
    print("OVERLOAD")
```
The spec says a reading *at or above* `5000` counts as overload, but this code lets `demand_kw = 5000` fall through to a lower branch. Find the operator that's wrong and fix it.

reflection.summary: Boundary operators matter more than they look.
reflection.content: Where else in the AI Energy OS will a `>` vs `>=` mistake matter more than it seems right now — think about safety limits, not just demand.

teachBack.summary: Explain single-branch execution to a junior engineer.
teachBack.content: Explain why only one branch of an if/elif/else chain ever runs, even if multiple conditions would technically be true, to someone who's never seen the construct before.

miniProject.summary: Add a classification step to the sensor-intake mini project.
miniProject.content: Extend the sensor-intake mini project from the Variables lesson: after computing `demand_mw`, classify the reading into NORMAL/WARNING/OVERLOAD and print the classification alongside the existing status line.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This threshold check becomes the first safety guard in the ingestion path.
portfolioEvidence.content: This classification step becomes the first safety guard in the Digital Twin's ingestion path, sitting right after the variable assignments committed in the Variables lesson.

labs.title: Threshold Classifier
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise providing a set of boundary-case demand readings (including values exactly at each threshold) that the learner's classifier must handle correctly.

simulations.title: Threshold Branch Visualizer
simulations.description: Interactive slider showing which if/elif/else branch fires as a demand value changes, including at exact boundary values.

flashcards.front: In an if/elif/else chain, how many branches execute at most?
flashcards.back: Exactly one — Python stops at the first true condition and skips the rest, even if a later condition would also be true.

flashcards.front: Why use >= instead of > when checking a safety limit like 'at or above 5000 kW is overload'?
flashcards.back: Because > would let a reading exactly equal to the limit slip through as safe, when the specification says the limit itself already counts as unsafe.

rubric: Correctly identifies the boundary operator bug (> vs >=) and explains why it matters, not just that the fix works
Fixed classifier correctly handles values exactly at each threshold, not just clearly-inside-a-band values
Independent coding task uses if/elif/else correctly without redundant or unreachable branches

references.title: Python Language Reference — The if statement
